title: A new Era: A Late-2025 Retrospective
author: Geby Jaff
summary: Analyzing the development of Ai by the end of 2025
content:
# The Year the Machines Learned to Think

You probably missed it.

Most people are still thinking about AI like it's 2023. They poked ChatGPT once, got bored, and went back to their spreadsheets. They have no idea what happened in the second half of 2025.

Something fundamental shifted. The machines learned to reason.

## Mathematics Falls

July 2025: Google's Gemini 2.5 Deep Think and an OpenAI experimental model earned gold medals at the International Mathematical Olympiad. They solved 5 out of 6 problems under strict competition conditions. Four and a half hours, no internet, no external tools. You can't bullshit your way through IMO proofs. For decades, this made math the fortress AI couldn't touch.

The breakthrough came from process supervision, getting feedback on each reasoning step and building "trees of thought" that explore multiple paths simultaneously. When one branch fails, they backtrack. This is exactly how expert mathematicians work.

November 2025: DeepSeek released Math-V2, scoring 118 out of 120 on the Putnam competition. The Putnam is harder than the IMO. The median score is typically zero or one. The top human that year scored 90 points.

Around the same time, Axioms AI got 8 out of 12 problems on the 2025 Putnam using Lean, a formal verification system. Given more time, they hit 9 out of 12. Lean is a programming language for mathematics. If the code compiles, the proof is valid. Zero probability of error.


DeepSeek built a dual system: a generator proposes proofs, a verifier criticizes them. The verifier scrutinizes logical flow, identifies gaps, and scores rigor. The generator refines based on that feedback. The model acts as its own harshest critic.

In frontier science, 2025 was also the year AIs stopped just “acing competitions” and started nudging the edge of what we consider discovery. GPT-5 helped Mehtaab Sawhney and Mark Sellke finish a decades-old Erdős problem by proposing the key final idea they were missing, while HarmonicMath’s *Aristotle* system independently produced a vetted proof of a simplified Erdős Problem #123 that had been open for about 30 years. DeepMind’s AlphaEvolve — an LLM-guided search engine for math — was turned loose on 67 problems across analysis, combinatorics, geometry, and number theory, rediscovering known results and, in several cases, improving on the best human constructions. Similar systems were reported to crack a long-standing puzzle in the Potts model from statistical physics, and on the biology side a Gemma-based model called C2S-Scale suggested a novel silmitasertib-plus-interferon drug combo that turns “cold” tumors “hot,” revealing a new, experimentally validated pathway for cancer immunotherapy.

## Coding, Agents, and World Building

September 2025: AI systems from Google and OpenAI dominated the ICPC World Finals. Gemini 2.5 Deep Think earned gold. OpenAI reportedly scored perfectly: 12 out of 12 problems. Every human team failed Problem C, a complex optimization challenge. Gemini solved it by combining dynamic programming with nested ternary searches.

But the real story is what happened in the terminal. Claude Code on the web allowed developers to delegate coding tasks directly from their browsers. The tool runs in isolated cloud environments, handles multiple parallel tasks, and integrates with GitHub. Anthropic reported a 5.5x increase in Claude Code revenue since the Claude 4 launch in May. By November, Claude Code reached $1 billion in run-rate revenue, just six months after launch and OpenAI engineers now merge 70% more pull requests weekly using their tool (codex).


The breakthrough in virtual worlds came from Google's Genie 3, released in August. This world model generates interactive 3D environments from text prompts at 24 frames per second in 720p. You can navigate these worlds in real time, and they maintain consistency for several minutes. DeepMind positions this as a crucial step toward AGI, using it to train agents in unlimited, generated scenarios.

OpenAI's Sora 2, released in September, mastered physics simulation for video generation. It generates video with synchronized audio and models physical reality accurately. When a basketball player misses a shot, the ball rebounds off the backboard rather than magically teleporting. OpenAI explicitly frames this as building world simulators that deeply understand physical reality.

## Time Horizons Doubling Every Seven Months

METR (Model Evaluation and Threat Research) introduced a new metric in March 2025: the 50% time horizon. This measures the length of tasks (measured by how long they take human professionals) that AI agents can complete autonomously with 50% reliability. The results are stark.

The time horizon has been doubling approximately every seven months since 2019. As of early 2025, Claude 3.7 Sonnet reached about 54 minutes. By late 2025, the numbers exploded.

OpenAI's 5.1 Codex Max reached 2 hours and 42 minutes.

This result is massive. Most jobs don't require planning or execution on time horizons beyond a month. If these results generalize beyond coding tasks, we are approaching a threshold where AI can handle substantial portions of knowledge work. Extrapolating the current trend suggests AI systems capable of month-long tasks with 50% reliability by 2027-2029, though uncertainty remains high.

## The ARC-AGI Breakthrough

The ARC-AGI benchmark measures fluid intelligence through abstract reasoning tasks that are easy for humans but historically impossible for AI. The benchmark tests genuine problem-solving ability rather than memorized knowledge.

The new ARC-AGI-2, released in 2025, made the benchmark even harder. Yet this year saw dramatic progress. Claude Opus 4.5 with 64k thinking achieved 37.6% accuracy. The top refinement solution, built on Gemini 3 Pro, scored 54%. These systems finally crossed the 50% threshold that seemed unreachable just months ago.

For context, humans average 60% on ARC-AGI-2. The gap is closing. The $1 million Grand Prize requires 85% accuracy with high efficiency.

## The Model Landscape

OpenAI's GPT-5.1 introduced distinct modes in November. GPT-5.1 Thinking implements adaptive reasoning, allocating inference time to "think" before responding. This is inference-time compute scaling: letting models think longer during use rather than making them bigger during training.

Google's Gemini 3 Deep Think achieved 41% on "Humanity's Last Exam," a benchmark designed to test absolute limits. Gemini 3 also introduced Generative UI, dynamically creating interactive widgets in response to queries.

The gap between proprietary and open-weights models essentially vanished. Deepseek's deepseek v3.2  rivals GPT-5 and Gemini 3.

## Embodiment and Infrastructure

Figure AI unveiled Figure 03 in October, featuring soft tactile sensors and designed for mass manufacturability. Target: 100,000 units. 1X Technologies launched pre-orders for Neo, priced around $20,000, with tendon-driven actuation for safe human interaction.

As model capabilities exploded, energy became the primary constraint. Google's Project Suncatcher proposes satellite clusters with TPUs in low Earth orbit. Solar panels in sun-synchronous orbit generate 8x more power than on Earth. The vacuum of space enables passive radiative cooling. Prototype launches target 2027.

## The Job Market Reality

While AI capabilities surged, the tech job market collapsed. 1.1 million workers were laid off in 2025, a number worse than the 2020 pandemic crash.

It is a restructuring. The sentiment on forums like Blind and Reddit has turned black, with users comparing the current environment to the Great Depression. Software engineers, product managers, and technical program managers were hit hardest.

The disparity between disciplines is now undeniable. Computer science graduates faced 6.1% unemployment in 2025, nearly double the rate of philosophy majors at 3.2%. Even graduates from MIT, Stanford, Carnegie Mellon, and UC Berkeley struggled. The share of graduates employed as engineers at major tech companies dropped from 25% in 2022 to just 11-12% in recent years. CS degrees more than doubled from 51,696 in 2013-2014 to 112,720 in 2022-2023, flooding the market exactly as demand vanished.

The irony is brutal. For over a decade, tech executives promoted computer science as a guaranteed path to prosperity. Now those same companies use AI and cost-cutting to eliminate the entry-level jobs they encouraged students to pursue. The historical safety net of a degree has evaporated. The Federal Reserve reported that the advantage college graduates historically held in finding jobs quickly is "becoming a thing of the past," with their job-finding rates declining to match those of workers with only a high school degree. A Berkeley professor noted that even outstanding students with 4.0 GPAs are receiving zero job offers, a trend he suspects is "irreversible".

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell confirmed the severity of the shift, stating that the "AI hiring apocalypse is real" and noting that "Job creation is pretty close to zero".

The impact reaches the highest tiers of the corporate world. Top consultancy firms—McKinsey, BCG, and Bain—are freezing starting salaries explicitly due to AI. The sentiment on industry forums has turned black, with users comparing the current environment to the Great Depression.


## What This Means

The conversation around AI in 2025 is stuck in the wrong frame. People ask "Can AI replace jobs?"

The real question is: what happens when reasoning itself becomes abundant?

We built an economy around scarcity of cognitive labor. Doctors, lawyers, engineers, and programmers command high wages because reasoning is scarce, because thinking is hard, because expertise takes years to develop.

What happens when it isn't?

title: Our Methodology: How We Track AI 2027 Predictions
author: Tracker Admin
summary: A deep dive into the process, criteria, and tools we use to evaluate the AI 2027 scenario predictions.
content:
This post explains our methodology for tracking and evaluating predictions from the AI 2027 scenario. We aim for transparency and objectivity in our assessment process.

## Prediction Extraction & Categorization

We carefully extract concrete, falsifiable predictions from the scenario text. Each prediction is:
- Categorized by topic (e.g., Compute, Agent Capabilities, Geopolitics)
- Assigned to a specific timeline segment
- Given a current status (e.g., Confirmed Accurate, Partially Accurate, Inaccurate, In Progress)
- Scored for accuracy when sufficient evidence exists

## Accuracy Scoring Rubric

We use a detailed rubric to ensure consistent evaluation across predictions. Scores range from 0-100% in 20% increments, based on four key criteria:
- Core Event/Claim: Did the main event or claim occur?
- Timing: Did it happen within the predicted timeframe?
- Magnitude/Scale: Was the scale/impact as predicted?
- Context/Nuance: Did surrounding circumstances align?

### Detailed Scoring Framework

| Score | Label | Criteria | Example |
|-------|--------|----------|----------|
| 100%  | Highly Accurate / Confirmed            | The prediction matched the observed reality with high fidelity across key criteria: core event, timing, magnitude/scale, and context/nuance. Any deviations are minor and do not detract from the prediction's overall correctness and insight.                                                                                                                               | Predicted a 10,000-person protest in DC in Late 2026 focused on AI job loss; a protest of ~10,500 occurred in Nov 2026 with that focus. |
| 80%   | Largely Accurate / Directionally Strong | The core event/claim occurred in a clearly recognizable form, capturing the general direction or essence of the prediction well. This indicates significant foresight. However, there were noticeable deviations in either the specific timing (e.g., happened 6-12 months later/earlier) or the magnitude/scale/specific details (e.g., different specific actors involved, significantly smaller/larger scale, different but related technology version). The prediction was clearly insightful and more right than wrong, despite inaccuracies in specifics. | Predicted China steals Agent-2 weights in Feb 2027. Instead, they stole Agent-1+ weights in April 2027. (Core event type - state-level theft of advanced model - occurred, capturing the essence. Timing and specific agent version differ = 80%). |
| 60%   | Partially Accurate / Mixed Results     | The prediction identified a relevant trend or event, but with significant inaccuracies in multiple key areas (e.g., both timing and magnitude were substantially off, or the core event happened but in a very different context or with different key players than predicted). While not entirely wrong, the prediction had substantial flaws that limit its overall accuracy and insightfulness.                                  | Predicted widespread consumer adoption of Agent X by Mid-2025 costing $100/month. Some niche adoption occurred by Late 2026 costing $500/month, but it wasn't widespread. (Trend related, but timing, scale, and cost were all significantly off). |
| 40%   | Partially Inaccurate / Weakly Related  | Some elements occurred, but major aspects did not, or were substantially different in nature. The core event might be vaguely related, but timing, magnitude, and context were all significantly off. OR, the prediction captured a minor related event but missed the main predicted phenomenon entirely. The directional insight was weak or misleading.                                                | Predicted 30% market growth driven primarily by AI; growth was 5%, driven by unrelated factors, with AI having negligible impact. |
| 20%   | Largely Inaccurate                     | Only a very minor, tangential, or superficial element of the prediction occurred. The core event/claim was incorrect, and the timing, magnitude, and context were significantly off. The prediction failed to capture the essence of what happened.                                                                                                                                                  | Predicted specific AI regulation Y passes; completely different regulation Z with opposite goals passes instead. |
| 0%    | Inaccurate / Debunked                  | The core event or claim did not happen. Events directly and fundamentally contradict the prediction. The prediction was wrong in its basic premise or outcome.                                                                                                                                                                                 | Predicted Company X dominates AI; Company X went bankrupt. |

## Important Notes

### Evidence Requirements
All evaluations must be based on verifiable evidence from credible sources:
- News reports
- Company announcements
- Market data
- Research papers
- Other reliable public sources

### Score Evolution
- Scores may change as new information becomes available
- Predictions marked "In Progress" are regularly re-evaluated
- Some predictions may be too early to score (marked as "Pending" or "Too Early to Tell")

### Handling Subjectivity
While scoring inevitably involves some judgment, especially for qualitative predictions, we strive for consistency by:
- Following the rubric criteria strictly
- Documenting our reasoning
- Linking to supporting evidence
- Updating assessments when significant new information emerges
